title: How Slow Apps Lose Users
intro: Your e-commerce app's conversion rate just dropped 30% overnight. It's like a store whose front door suddenly takes 5 seconds to open -- customers walk away before they even get inside. Sales are plummeting but nothing in the code changed. Your CEO is asking what happened. Time to investigate.

teach.title: Speed = Trust
teach.body: Imagine walking into two stores. One has automatic doors that open instantly. The other has a sticky door that takes 5 seconds to open. Which store feels more professional? Which one do you trust with your credit card? **Speed is trust**. Users subconsciously judge your app's quality by how fast it responds.

teach.title: Speed = The Checkout Line
teach.body: Picture two grocery stores. One has 10 checkout lanes open and you're out in 2 minutes. The other has 1 lane open with a huge line. You might abandon your full cart and walk out. That's exactly what happens online -- users have a mental checkout line, and if it takes too long, they leave their cart behind and go to a competitor who's faster.

teach.title: The Numbers Are Brutal
teach.body:
Research consistently shows:

- **53%** of mobile users abandon a site that takes over 3 seconds to load
- Each extra second of load time can reduce conversions by **7%**
- A 100ms delay in response time can reduce conversion rates by **7%**
- Users who experience slow loads are **less likely to return**, even after the problem is fixed

teach.title: The Bounce Rate Death Spiral
teach.body:
Slow pages create a vicious cycle:

1. Page loads slowly -> users bounce
2. High bounce rate -> Google ranks you lower
3. Lower ranking -> less traffic
4. Less traffic -> less revenue to fix the problem

Performance isn't a nice-to-have -- it directly affects your business survival.
Every second your app takes to load is money walking out the door. When you vibecode a fast app, you're not just writing better code -- you're directly protecting your revenue stream.

Speed equals trust and money. Over half of users abandon slow sites, and every extra second of load time costs you conversions. Performance problems compound: slow pages lose users, rankings, and revenue.

start.narrative: Your e-commerce site's conversion rate dropped 30% overnight. Revenue is down significantly. The development team says no code was deployed. Where do you start investigating?

choices.text: Check page load times in your monitoring tools (RUM dashboard, Lighthouse, etc.)
Look at the server error logs for crashes
Check if a competitor launched a sale

check-speed.narrative: You open your monitoring dashboard and see it: average page load time jumped from 1.8 seconds to 7.2 seconds yesterday. The site isn't broken -- it's just painfully slow. Where in the request chain is the time going?

choices.text: Open the Network tab in DevTools to see which specific requests are slow
Check if the database is slow
Assume it's a DDoS attack and enable rate limiting

check-errors.narrative: No server errors or crashes. The site is technically working fine -- all pages return 200 OK responses. But something is making users leave without buying. Maybe the issue isn't errors but speed?

choices.text: Check the site's loading speed in monitoring tools

check-competitor.narrative: No major competitor activity. Your traffic numbers are actually the same as usual -- people are visiting. They're just not buying. The problem is on your end.

choices.text: Check if the site is performing differently than usual

find-cause.narrative: Network tab tells the story: HTML and API responses come back in under 200ms, but every product image is taking 5+ seconds and they're all served from cdn.imagekit.io. You check status.imagekit.io and see a partial outage notice. What's your immediate move?

choices.text: Flip your image origin to a backup CDN (Cloudflare, Bunny, or your fallback) to restore speed within minutes
Open a support ticket with the CDN provider and wait for them to recover
Email all customers a coupon code to make up for the slowness

harden-frontend.narrative: The backup CDN is serving images and load times dropped to 2.4 seconds. Still slower than ideal. What frontend change reduces the impact of a slow CDN even when one is degraded?

choices.text: Add 'loading="lazy"' to images below the fold and serve responsive 'srcset' sizes so mobile downloads less data
Replace all tags with CSS background-images to defer loading
Remove images from product cards entirely until the CDN recovers

set-monitoring.narrative: Lazy loading shipped, p75 load time is back to 1.9 seconds. Today you found out about the slowdown from the conversion-rate dashboard. You don't want to learn about the next one from revenue numbers. What do you set up?

choices.text: Configure RUM alerts that page on-call when p75 LCP exceeds 3s for 10 minutes, plus synthetic checks against the CDN every minute
Add a Slack channel where the team manually posts Lighthouse scores once a week
Trust the conversion-rate dashboard to keep flagging it like it did this time

post-mortem.narrative: Monitoring is in place. Final decision: what do you do with what you learned today?

choices.text: Write a short post-mortem documenting the timeline, root cause, fixes, and a list of follow-ups (define an SLO, add a second CDN to the build, audit other third-party deps)
Mention what happened in standup tomorrow and move on
Nothing -- the site is fast again, no need to dwell on it

end-good.narrative: You diagnosed from RUM data, located the slow request in the network tab, mitigated the CDN outage with a fallback, hardened the frontend with lazy loading and responsive images, set up active monitoring so the next regression triggers a page within minutes, and wrote a post-mortem with concrete follow-ups. Conversions are back -- and the team is now meaningfully more resilient than yesterday.
end-good.feedback: This is mature performance engineering. Speed of detection (monitoring) and speed of mitigation (a hot CDN swap) matter as much as the original performance work. The post-mortem is what turns a one-time fire into permanent improvement -- without it, the same shape of incident hits again next quarter.

end-okay.narrative: You restored speed, lazy-loaded images, and set up alerts. Skipping the written post-mortem means the lessons stay in your head -- next time a different person is on-call, they'll have to re-discover the playbook from scratch.
end-okay.feedback: Strong incident response. The post-mortem feels like extra work but it's the highest-leverage 30 minutes of the whole incident: it captures the timeline, the root cause, and the follow-ups in a place the next on-call can read. Without it, you're rebuilding the same mental model every outage.

end-weak.narrative: Speed is restored and alerts are set, but no one outside this incident knows what happened. The next CDN outage may take longer to mitigate because the playbook lives only in your head. The follow-ups (second CDN, SLO definition, third-party audit) won't happen because they were never written down.
end-weak.feedback: You stabilized the site, which is the most important thing. The miss is the lack of documentation: a five-paragraph post-mortem turns this incident into a permanent capability for the team. Without it, the next slow third party will reset the clock on response time.
